Real Analysis

Nested Intervals and the Foundations of Sequences

Nested Intervals and the Foundations of Sequences

Intervals

Nested Intervals

Having classified what intervals look like in the previous lecture, we now ask a dynamic question: if we shrink through an infinite chain of intervals, each contained in the last, what — if anything — survives in the intersection?

Nested Intervals

A sequence of intervals (In)n∈N(I_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}(In​)n∈N​ is nested (decreasing) if

In+1⊆In⊆⋯⊆I2⊆I1.I_{n+1} \subseteq I_n \subseteq \cdots \subseteq I_2 \subseteq I_1.In+1​⊆In​⊆⋯⊆I2​⊆I1​.

We are interested in the intersection

⋂n=1∞In  =  ?\bigcap_{n=1}^\infty I_n \;=\;?n=1⋂∞​In​=?

Examples: Open vs. Closed Endpoints

The answer turns out to depend delicately on whether the intervals are open or closed — even though the two examples below are nested in exactly the same “shrinking” way.

Example: Closedness Matters
In=(0,1n)⇒(In) is nested,⋂n=1∞In=∅.In=[0,1n]⇒(In) is nested,⋂n=1∞In={0}.\begin{aligned} & I_n = \bigl(0,\tfrac{1}{n}\bigr) \quad \Rightarrow \quad (I_n) \text{ is nested}, \quad \bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} I_n = \varnothing. \\ & I_n = \bigl[0,\tfrac{1}{n}\bigr] \quad \Rightarrow \quad (I_n) \text{ is nested}, \quad \bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} I_n = \{0\}. \end{aligned}​In​=(0,n1​)⇒(In​) is nested,n=1⋂∞​In​=∅.In​=[0,n1​]⇒(In​) is nested,n=1⋂∞​In​={0}.​

This shows that to guarantee a nonempty intersection, closed endpoints can be essential: dropping the single point 000 from each interval in the first family is enough to empty out the intersection entirely.

Boundedness Is Also Needed

Closedness alone is not sufficient, however — the intervals must also be bounded, or the same failure can occur for a different reason.

Closedness Alone Is Not Enough

Consider In=[n,∞)I_n = [n,\infty)In​=[n,∞), which are closed and nested (decreasing). Then

⋂n=1∞In=∅,\bigcap_{n=1}^\infty I_n = \varnothing,n=1⋂∞​In​=∅,

since by the Archimedean Property, for any a∈Ra \in \mathbb{R}a∈R there exists n∈Nn \in \mathbb{N}n∈N with a<na < na<n, hence a∉[n,∞)a \notin [n,\infty)a∈/[n,∞). So the classical nested intervals result requires both closedness and boundedness.

Nested Intervals Property

With both necessary conditions identified, we now show they are also sufficient — closed, bounded, nested intervals always have nonempty intersection, and in fact that intersection is itself a closed interval built from a supremum and an infimum.

Nested Intervals Property

Let In=[an,bn]I_n = [a_n, b_n]In​=[an​,bn​] be a nested sequence of nonempty closed and bounded intervals in R\mathbb{R}R:

[an+1,bn+1]⊆[an,bn]for all n.[a_{n+1},b_{n+1}] \subseteq [a_n,b_n] \quad \text{for all } n.[an+1​,bn+1​]⊆[an​,bn​]for all n.

Then the intersection is nonempty:

⋂n=1∞In≠∅.\bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} I_n \neq \varnothing.n=1⋂∞​In​=∅.

In fact, if A={an:n∈N}A = \{a_n : n\in\mathbb{N}\}A={an​:n∈N} and B={bn:n∈N}B = \{b_n : n\in\mathbb{N}\}B={bn​:n∈N}, then

α:=sup⁡A≤inf⁡B=:β,⋂n=1∞In=[α,β].\alpha := \sup A \le \inf B =: \beta, \qquad \bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} I_n = [\alpha,\beta].α:=supA≤infB=:β,n=1⋂∞​In​=[α,β].
Proof

Since an≤bn≤b1a_n \le b_n \le b_1an​≤bn​≤b1​ for all nnn, the set A={an}A = \{a_n\}A={an​} is nonempty and bounded above; by completeness, ξ:=sup⁡A\xi := \sup Aξ:=supA exists.

Goal: ξ≤bn\xi \le b_nξ≤bn​ for all nnn. Suppose, for contradiction, that bm<ξb_m < \xibm​<ξ for some mmm. Then:

{if i≤m,ai≤am≤bm (nestedness),if i>m,nestedness gives bi≤bm, and ai≤bi≤bm.\begin{cases} \text{if } i \le m, & a_i \le a_m \le b_m \text{ (nestedness)},\\[2pt] \text{if } i > m, & \text{nestedness gives } b_i \le b_m,\text{ and } a_i \le b_i \le b_m. \end{cases}{if i≤m,if i>m,​ai​≤am​≤bm​ (nestedness),nestedness gives bi​≤bm​, and ai​≤bi​≤bm​.​

Either way, ai≤bma_i \le b_mai​≤bm​ for every iii, so bmb_mbm​ is an upper bound of AAA — contradicting ξ=sup⁡A>bm\xi = \sup A > b_mξ=supA>bm​. Hence ξ≤bn\xi \le b_nξ≤bn​ for all nnn. Also an≤ξa_n \le \xian​≤ξ for all nnn, directly from ξ=sup⁡A\xi = \sup Aξ=supA. So ξ∈In\xi \in I_nξ∈In​ for every nnn, giving ξ∈⋂n=1∞In\xi \in \bigcap_{n=1}^\infty I_nξ∈⋂n=1∞​In​.

Finally, let α:=sup⁡A\alpha := \sup Aα:=supA and β:=inf⁡B\beta := \inf Bβ:=infB (this is exactly the “comparing sup⁡A\sup AsupA and inf⁡B\inf BinfB” situation from Lecture 4, since an≤bma_n \le b_man​≤bm​ for all n,mn,mn,m, so α≤β\alpha \le \betaα≤β). For any xxx with α≤x≤β\alpha \le x \le \betaα≤x≤β, we have an≤α≤x≤β≤bna_n \le \alpha \le x \le \beta \le b_nan​≤α≤x≤β≤bn​ for every nnn, so x∈Inx \in I_nx∈In​ for all nnn. Hence the intersection equals exactly [α,β][\alpha,\beta][α,β].

The supremum-infimum gap β−α\beta - \alphaβ−α measures how much “room” is left in the intersection. When that room shrinks to nothing, the limiting set must collapse to a single point.

Uniqueness Under Vanishing Length

If In=[an,bn]I_n = [a_n,b_n]In​=[an​,bn​] are nested and

inf⁡n∈N(bn−an)=0(equivalently, lim⁡n→∞(bn−an)=0),\inf_{n\in\mathbb{N}}(b_n - a_n) = 0 \qquad \bigl(\text{equivalently, } \lim_{n\to\infty}(b_n-a_n)=0\bigr),n∈Ninf​(bn​−an​)=0(equivalently, n→∞lim​(bn​−an​)=0),

then the intersection is a singleton:

⋂n=1∞In={ξ},for a unique ξ∈R.\bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} I_n = \{\xi\}, \quad \text{for a unique } \xi \in \mathbb{R}.n=1⋂∞​In​={ξ},for a unique ξ∈R.
Why vanishing length forces a single point

If β−α>0\beta - \alpha > 0β−α>0, that gap itself would be a lower bound on bn−anb_n - a_nbn​−an​ for every nnn — for example, bn−an≥β−αb_n - a_n \ge \beta - \alphabn​−an​≥β−α always holds — contradicting inf⁡(bn−an)=0\inf(b_n-a_n)=0inf(bn​−an​)=0. So α=β\alpha = \betaα=β, and [α,β][\alpha,\beta][α,β] collapses to the single point {ξ}\{\xi\}{ξ}.

Sequences

Definition

The nested-intervals construction naturally produced a sequence of endpoints (an)(a_n)(an​) and (bn)(b_n)(bn​). We now make the notion of “sequence” itself precise, as the central object of study for the rest of the course.

Sequence

A sequence of real numbers is a function

X:N→R,n↦X(n)=xn.X : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{R}, \qquad n \mapsto X(n) = x_n.X:N→R,n↦X(n)=xn​.

Notation: XXX, (xn)(x_n)(xn​), or {xn:n∈N}\{x_n : n \in \mathbb{N}\}{xn​:n∈N}.

Examples:

  • ((−1)n)\bigl((-1)^n\bigr)((−1)n)
  • (1n)\bigl(\tfrac{1}{n}\bigr)(n1​)
  • (12n)\bigl(\tfrac{1}{2n}\bigr)(2n1​)

Some sequences are defined recursively — for instance, the Fibonacci sequence:

x1=1,x2=1,xn+2=xn+1+xn(n∈N).x_1 = 1, \quad x_2 = 1, \quad x_{n+2} = x_{n+1} + x_n \quad (n \in \mathbb{N}).x1​=1,x2​=1,xn+2​=xn+1​+xn​(n∈N).

Limit of a Sequence

Convergence

The central question about a sequence is whether its terms settle down near some fixed value as nnn grows. The formal definition captures “settles down near” using the same ε\varepsilonε-language that appeared in the Supremum Characterizations of Lecture 4.

Convergence

A sequence (xn)(x_n)(xn​) converges to x∈Rx \in \mathbb{R}x∈R if for every ϵ>0\epsilon > 0ϵ>0 there exists N(ϵ)∈NN(\epsilon) \in \mathbb{N}N(ϵ)∈N such that for all n≥N(ϵ)n \ge N(\epsilon)n≥N(ϵ),

∣xn−x∣<ϵ.|x_n - x| < \epsilon.∣xn​−x∣<ϵ.

Equivalently, for all sufficiently large nnn,

x−ϵ<xn<x+ϵ,x - \epsilon < x_n < x + \epsilon,x−ϵ<xn​<x+ϵ,

i.e. xnx_nxn​ lies in the ϵ\epsilonϵ-neighborhood Nϵ(x):=(x−ϵ,x+ϵ)N_\epsilon(x) := (x-\epsilon, x+\epsilon)Nϵ​(x):=(x−ϵ,x+ϵ).

Notation: lim⁡n→∞xn=x\lim_{n\to\infty} x_n = xlimn→∞​xn​=x, or xn→xx_n \to xxn​→x as n→∞n \to \inftyn→∞. If a limit exists, the sequence converges; otherwise it diverges.

Visualizing Convergence of an=1+(−1)nna_n = 1 + \frac{(-1)^n}{n}an​=1+n(−1)n​

For a given ϵ>0\epsilon > 0ϵ>0, there exists N(ϵ)N(\epsilon)N(ϵ) such that all ana_nan​ with n≥N(ϵ)n \ge N(\epsilon)n≥N(ϵ) lie in the band (L−ϵ,L+ϵ)(L-\epsilon, L+\epsilon)(L−ϵ,L+ϵ). This illustrates ∣an−L∣<ϵ|a_n - L| < \epsilon∣an​−L∣<ϵ for large nnn.

Uniqueness of Limits

Before computing any limits, it is worth confirming the definition behaves sensibly: a sequence cannot converge to two different values at once.

Limits Are Unique

If (xn)→L1(x_n) \to L_1(xn​)→L1​ and (xn)→L2(x_n) \to L_2(xn​)→L2​, then L1=L2L_1 = L_2L1​=L2​.

Proof

Suppose L1≠L2L_1 \neq L_2L1​=L2​, and set ϵ=12∣L1−L2∣>0\epsilon = \tfrac12 |L_1 - L_2| > 0ϵ=21​∣L1​−L2​∣>0. There exist N1,N2N_1, N_2N1​,N2​ such that ∣xn−L1∣<ϵ|x_n - L_1| < \epsilon∣xn​−L1​∣<ϵ for all n≥N1n \ge N_1n≥N1​, and ∣xn−L2∣<ϵ|x_n - L_2| < \epsilon∣xn​−L2​∣<ϵ for all n≥N2n \ge N_2n≥N2​. For n≥N:=max⁡{N1,N2}n \ge N := \max\{N_1, N_2\}n≥N:=max{N1​,N2​}, the triangle inequality (Lecture 3) gives

∣L1−L2∣≤∣L1−xn∣+∣xn−L2∣<ϵ+ϵ=∣L1−L2∣,|L_1 - L_2| \le |L_1 - x_n| + |x_n - L_2| < \epsilon + \epsilon = |L_1 - L_2|,∣L1​−L2​∣≤∣L1​−xn​∣+∣xn​−L2​∣<ϵ+ϵ=∣L1​−L2​∣,

a contradiction. Hence L1=L2L_1 = L_2L1​=L2​.

Examples of Convergence

The definition of convergence is existential in N(ϵ)N(\epsilon)N(ϵ) but must hold for every ϵ\epsilonϵ, so proving a limit typically means producing an explicit formula for N(ϵ)N(\epsilon)N(ϵ) — almost always via the Archimedean property.

Example: Convergence of (1n)\bigl(\tfrac{1}{n}\bigr)(n1​)

Claim: lim⁡n→∞1n=0\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{1}{n} = 0n→∞lim​n1​=0.

Let ϵ>0\epsilon > 0ϵ>0. By the Archimedean property, there exists N(ϵ)∈NN(\epsilon) \in \mathbb{N}N(ϵ)∈N with N(ϵ)>1ϵN(\epsilon) > \tfrac{1}{\epsilon}N(ϵ)>ϵ1​. Then for all n≥N(ϵ)n \ge N(\epsilon)n≥N(ϵ),

1n≤1N(ϵ)<ϵ,\frac{1}{n} \le \frac{1}{N(\epsilon)} < \epsilon,n1​≤N(ϵ)1​<ϵ,

i.e. ∣1n−0∣<ϵ\bigl|\tfrac1n - 0\bigr| < \epsilon​n1​−0​<ϵ. Therefore 1n→0\tfrac1n \to 0n1​→0.

A second example requires an algebraic trick — multiplying by the conjugate — to turn a difference of square roots into a form the Archimedean property can control.

Conjugate Trick

For a,b≥0a, b \ge 0a,b≥0,

a−b=(a−b)(a+b)a+b=a−ba+b.\sqrt{a} - \sqrt{b} = \frac{(\sqrt{a}-\sqrt{b})(\sqrt{a}+\sqrt{b})}{\sqrt{a}+\sqrt{b}} = \frac{a-b}{\sqrt{a}+\sqrt{b}}.a​−b​=a​+b​(a​−b​)(a​+b​)​=a​+b​a−b​.
Example: Convergence of (n+1−n)\bigl(\sqrt{n+1}-\sqrt{n}\bigr)(n+1​−n​)

Claim: lim⁡n→∞(n+1−n)=0\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}\bigl(\sqrt{n+1}-\sqrt{n}\bigr) = 0n→∞lim​(n+1​−n​)=0.

Applying the Conjugate Trick with a=n+1a = n+1a=n+1, b=nb = nb=n:

n+1−n=(n+1)−nn+1+n=1n+1+n≤12n.\sqrt{n+1}-\sqrt{n} = \frac{(n+1)-n}{\sqrt{n+1}+\sqrt{n}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{n+1}+\sqrt{n}} \le \frac{1}{2\sqrt{n}}.n+1​−n​=n+1​+n​(n+1)−n​=n+1​+n​1​≤2n​1​.

Given ϵ>0\epsilon > 0ϵ>0, by the Archimedean property there exists N(ϵ)∈NN(\epsilon) \in \mathbb{N}N(ϵ)∈N with N(ϵ)>(12ϵ)2N(\epsilon) > \bigl(\tfrac{1}{2\epsilon}\bigr)^2N(ϵ)>(2ϵ1​)2. Then for all n≥N(ϵ)n \ge N(\epsilon)n≥N(ϵ),

0≤n+1−n≤12n<ϵ,0 \le \sqrt{n+1}-\sqrt{n} \le \frac{1}{2\sqrt{n}} < \epsilon,0≤n+1​−n​≤2n​1​<ϵ,

so the sequence converges to 000.

Divergence

Convergence is a “for every ϵ\epsilonϵ” statement; negating it correctly means finding a single ϵ0\epsilon_0ϵ0​ that repeatedly fails, no matter how far out in the sequence we look.

Divergence

A sequence (xn)(x_n)(xn​) does not converge to x∈Rx \in \mathbb{R}x∈R if there exists ϵ0>0\epsilon_0 > 0ϵ0​>0 such that for every N∈NN \in \mathbb{N}N∈N there exists m≥Nm \ge Nm≥N with

∣xm−x∣≥ϵ0.|x_m - x| \ge \epsilon_0.∣xm​−x∣≥ϵ0​.

A sequence diverges (in R\mathbb{R}R) if there is no x∈Rx \in \mathbb{R}x∈R to which it converges.

Tails of Sequences

Since convergence only cares about eventual behavior, discarding any finite number of initial terms should have no effect on whether — or to what — a sequence converges. The next definition and lemma make this precise.

Tail of a Sequence

For a sequence X=(x1,x2,… )X = (x_1, x_2, \dots)X=(x1​,x2​,…) and m∈Nm \in \mathbb{N}m∈N, the mmm-tail of XXX is the sequence

Xm:={xm+n:n∈N}=(xm+1,xm+2,… ).X_m := \{x_{m+n} : n \in \mathbb{N}\} = (x_{m+1}, x_{m+2}, \dots).Xm​:={xm+n​:n∈N}=(xm+1​,xm+2​,…).
Tails Preserve Limits

XXX converges to xxx if and only if XmX_mXm​ converges to xxx for any (equivalently, for some) m∈Nm \in \mathbb{N}m∈N.

Proof

(⇒\Rightarrow⇒) If xn→xx_n \to xxn​→x, then for any ϵ>0\epsilon > 0ϵ>0 there exists N(ϵ)N(\epsilon)N(ϵ) such that ∣xn−x∣<ϵ|x_n - x| < \epsilon∣xn​−x∣<ϵ for all n≥N(ϵ)n \ge N(\epsilon)n≥N(ϵ). For the tail, take N′(ϵ):=max⁡{N(ϵ),m}N'(\epsilon) := \max\{N(\epsilon), m\}N′(ϵ):=max{N(ϵ),m}; then ∣xm+n−x∣<ϵ|x_{m+n} - x| < \epsilon∣xm+n​−x∣<ϵ whenever m+n≥N′(ϵ)m + n \ge N'(\epsilon)m+n≥N′(ϵ).

(⇐\Leftarrow⇐) If xm+n→xx_{m+n} \to xxm+n​→x, then for any ϵ>0\epsilon > 0ϵ>0 there exists K(ϵ)K(\epsilon)K(ϵ) such that ∣xm+n−x∣<ϵ|x_{m+n} - x| < \epsilon∣xm+n​−x∣<ϵ for all n≥K(ϵ)n \ge K(\epsilon)n≥K(ϵ). Set N(ϵ):=m+K(ϵ)N(\epsilon) := m + K(\epsilon)N(ϵ):=m+K(ϵ). Then for any k≥N(ϵ)k \ge N(\epsilon)k≥N(ϵ), write k=m+nk = m+nk=m+n with n≥K(ϵ)n \ge K(\epsilon)n≥K(ϵ), so ∣xk−x∣<ϵ|x_k - x| < \epsilon∣xk​−x∣<ϵ. Therefore xn→xx_n \to xxn​→x.

Why tails matter going forward

This lemma is what justifies phrases like “for all sufficiently large nnn” throughout convergence proofs: it doesn’t matter which tail you look at, or how many terms you throw away at the start — convergence and its limit are entirely tail properties. This will be used repeatedly once sequences are combined algebraically (sums, products, quotients) in later lectures.
